sentences.text: My name is Eli Stern, S-T-E-R-N.
The second name was the same.
The first name was spelled E-L-I-A-S.
Petrova in Romania.
Leah, L-E-A-H, Perl, P-E-R-L.
Mordechai Hersh.
She was a beautiful lady, and that she was a housewife.
Her only thing in life was to take care of her husband and her children.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] When-- after they-- after they were married for about a year, they moved to Stramtura, which was a town not far from the place that I was born in.
This village that we lived in is a northern Transylvania, called-- the area is called Maramures, and the closest city to us was Sighet.
We lived a-- it was in the Carpathian Mountains.
From-- after I was a year old I lived in Stramtura until the day that we were taken into the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My father comes from a family of 10 children.
He was-- he was the oldest of the 10.
When the children were still young, his father died of pneumonia.
And when these-- when the-- every child that got married after that and they had a child and if the child was a boy, the boy was named after my grandfather Eli, the one that I'm named after.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We lived in a-- in a-- at a time where when two people got married a dowry was given.
My mother-- my mother gave a dowry of money.
My mother-- my father's mother gave my father land because we were all farmers.
I remember my father building our house, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] which took a long time.
Took several years before the house was finished.
In the meantime, my mother had other children.
I had-- we were five children-- four brothers and a sister.
We lived in a town that it was-- we didn't have many luxuries, but we were very content and happy because we were living together with family.
The Stern family moved into Stramtura in 1773.
And I figured out somehow that I'm the eighth generation since they moved in to Stramtura.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As I say, the, the town was a-- most people in the town were farmers, and some people were merchants.
We had several candy stores or grocery stores, and that was about the, the extent of the commercial part of the town.
People grew up in the same house.
Somebody got married, they lived in the town.
Subsequently to that, we were all very close.
We all knew each other.
We were about 83 Jewish families in this town.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We-- the life in the town was simple, but very content.
We didn't have much, but we really didn't need much.
We all had each other and we lived-- if we-- anybody needed anything, it was just a matter of going next door or across the street to grandma or to aunt or uncle and borrow whatever.
We had our own fruits and vegetables from our own garden.
We had our own corn and wheat because we had our own farm, and we had our own stuff.
There were two mills in the town.
When we were cutting wheat or collecting the corn from the fields, we went to the mill and we had-- it was ground up into flour.
Our, our mother-- there was no bakery in the town.
Our mother did all the baking and the cooking.
We did not have to go out and buy flour.
We didn't have to go out and buy fruits and vegetables because we had it for our own garden.
So it was-- it was a nice life.
My father told me that when he was a young man, he was in the Romanian army, and his brother next to him, the second, second youngest to him as well.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were very little anti-Semitism at, at the point when I was two, three, four, five years old.
We lived with our neighbors.
We didn't have-- we didn't have any problems with each other.
I went to-- when I went into first grade, I went into, to public school part of the day.
And part of the day, I went to cheder.
We come from a Orthodox Hasidic family.
My father used to visit the vizhnitz or rebbe.
Most-- we only had one shul in the town.
The married men or the married ladies-- the married men all had shtreimels and the married ladies all wore sheitels.
It was an Orthodox town.
We had a yeshiva in our town.
The rabbi of the Yeshiva and of our town-- his name was Hersh Kahan, K-A-H-A-N.
There were about approximately 100 yeshiva boys from the neighboring towns that came into the yeshiva to our town.
It was pretty much a, a life that aside from our fathers who were out working hard, we just lived in harmony with our friends and with our neighbors.
Yes.
The rebbe in our town was the, the man that we all went to for advice.

If somebody went into a business or if somebody wanted to go and take a trip or if there was a question as to something as kosher or not or whatever, he was the man that we all respected, and we all listened to.
The only experience that I had with him was when I went to cheder, he-- periodically, he would visit our classroom, and he would listen to us and sort of test us to see if-- how we were doing.
As-- his son was in my classroom, as a matter of fact, and later on I will talk about him because the rebbe and his son were in the-- were in the war with me in the camps.
It's Zanvyl.
The Yiddish name was Zanvyl, and the English name is Sam.
He lives in Israel today.
When the men in the town were busy making a living and so forth, the ladies in the town would be busy being matchmakers, would be busy talking about their children growing up.
And they were-- it was a very harmonious life between the-- between the neighbors in the town.
We-- we had-- the town had a city hall, a police station, and a post office.
About three times a day we had bus service that would pass through the town.
As I say, it was an agricultural town.
It was a lot of mostly farmers and merchants.
My father was a cattle man.
He dealt with our local farmers and also with people in the-- in the county.
For those standards my father did very well as a provider, and life was pretty much uneventful for-- till about 1940.
We-- I was a child.

I don't remember much, but if I remember the adults talking to us whatever happened in Germany and with Hitler and so on was so remote that it-- the adults were talking about it, but it didn't mean much to us.
We didn't know much about it.
And it really didn't mean much to us.
In 1940, we were occupied by Hungary.
Hungary was an ally at the time of Germany.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The part of northern Transylvania that I lived in was occupied by Hungarian army, and things started to change slowly in the town.
The remaining-- we had what they called Gendarme.
Instead of-- there were police, but they called them Gendarme, or the Hungarians called them [HUNGARIAN].
Part of their uniform was that they had feathers, and they had like feathers in their hat, like rooster feathers type.
It was part of their uniform.
As things got-- as things went on and we got into more of anti-Semitism, we had a nickname for them.
We call them the feathers.
Instead of saying the police are coming, we said the feathers are coming.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And after they occupied us we-- for a short while thereafter, I still went to the same public school, to the same cheder.
The difference was that public school was compulsory to change from Romanian that we grew up with that language into Hungarian language.
Slowly, things started to change.
Before where men of military age would, would be drafted into the army, now men of military age were drafted.
If they were non-Jewish, they went into the army.
The Jewish boys that were drafted went into a labor battalion.
They didn't wear any kind of uniform, but they had to wear a yellow armband.
And they were working labor.
It's exactly what it says, labor battalion.
They were working pick and shovel type of work.
So-- as we-- as time went on the kids that we grew up with and that are still in school with us, we had peyes.
I had peyes when I grew up.
Slowly, they would either make fun of us, pull the peyes, call us dirty Jew, why don't you go to Palestine?
We-- slowly, as things got more advanced in the-- in this-- in the anti-Semitism department.
Did you understand why people were treating you that way?
Did you talk about it at home with your family?
We sort of learned to live with it.
We just-- we just accepted it, and we were happy that nothing worse was happening to us.
So as time went on, we continued to go-- I continued going to school, and I continued to live with being called names.
But it was part of-- it got to that point where it was part of growing up.
We accepted it, and we-- there was nothing we could do about it.
Did you hear rumors about what was going on in Poland at that time?
We absolutely didn't hear anything that was going on in Poland.
We were very, very much in the dark.
Slowly, new laws were being implemented.
There were people that weren't born in, in our area, and they were called state-less.
They did not have a passport to show that they were citizens of the country.
Those people were rounded up, put on wagons, horse-drawn wagons within-- there was no railroad in our town.
They were put on wagons, and they were-- we saw them passing through our town.
We knew they were Jewish.
We knew they were being taken away.
There were people from other towns that were taken someplace, but we didn't know where.
We found out later that they were taken over the border to, to the Ukraine.
When they arrived in the Ukraine, they were mass-executed, and they were buried in mass graves.
We did not know that at the time.
When this-- one of the guys that was drafted into this labor battalion, one of the Jewish guys, his name was [?
Mechel ?] Steimetz.
He came home.
He was in the Ukraine, and he came home and told people about what was going on.
None of us believed him.
Nobody wanted to believe.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Life is going on in the town.
The new--
Not me personally.
I was a kid of 12, 13 at the time, but he was telling the adults and I heard adults talking about it.
That people actually thought that this man was out of his mind.
They did not believe that people would just kill in cold blood and execute people and just bury them in a mass grave.
People just didn't want to believe that.
OK.
I was still going to school and to cheder.
A new order came out from, from the what we called the feathers, the police, that all males 12 years and up have to one day a week report to the police station.

And at the police station we had to wear yellow arm bands, and we would made-- we were given picks and shovels, and we were made to work labor on the highway department, different work, whether they needed it or not, just a form of harassing.
So that was one day a week that we had to report.
They would call names to make sure that everybody is there, and there was some people that would skip and not show up certain times.
There were days that they would come to the house and just arrest a 12-year-old or older.
And he was put into the local jail for a couple of days, and he was let out.

And slowly things were happening where a man in the village-- mostly it would be a Hasidic man-- when they were going on the street, walking on the street, or going into another town just for fun they would shave a half a beard off him-- leave a half off just, just for fun.
And there was no recourse.
Nobody can go to report anything.

Nobody would-- there was not even any thought about going to the police because even though the-- our local population's there, not the police themselves, the, the people just took it and they said, thank God that's all that happened to me.
They always-- people always thought worse things than this can happen.
We don't know what can happen, but if they just cut half a beard off, we'll have to learn with it.
It happened to my uncle.
It didn't happen to anybody closer than that in my family that I can remember.
My father, being that he was a cattlemen, he knew a lot of people, a lot of Gentiles that he did a lot of business with them.
He was one of the most well-known people in the town, and there were many things that he was not picked on as much as some other people were picked on during that period.
Another, another law came out that Jewish merchants cannot be-- cannot do business anymore.
They did not get any certificates, as you would call.
Chamber of Commerce would not give them a license to do business.
Some of the Jewish businesspeople would get around that.
They took on a Gentile as a partner.
They would get around that, and that was-- it wasn't official.
And that was a way of making a living and stay in business.
In the meantime, as life is going on like that in our town we see people being deported from other towns that pass our town, and they're going north, but we don't know where.
Horse-drawn wagons.

Most of the time when this happened they picked a Shabbas where they knew that they can round everybody up, and that everybody is together because when, when it came to Shabbas, families came from afar.
Everybody came home.
Everybody was together.
We went to our local synagogue.
It was the only synagogue in town.
My father got dressed in the Hasidic garb.
He had a black coat and a shtreimel.
We came home from synagogue Friday night.
We sang [INAUDIBLE].
My mother was-- had fish, chicken.
She had a nice table set for us.
And it was a-- the only way that we knew what a Shabbas should look like.
And the same thing happened on Shabbas morning.
People walked to shul.
People talked to each other.
Most of the Jewish families lived on the main streets of the town, so it was a short walk to shul for most of us.
And people-- during the week people would dress in work clothes and the best that they can do.
But on Shabbas, they always managed to get dressed as much as possible in the best clothes that they had.
It was-- it was a sweet life.
It was a nice life.
We, we lived-- brothers and sisters were together.
Uncles and aunts and grandparents were in the same town, and it was-- it was a nice life.
I miss that life a lot.
